title: Visual Testing: Specific Applications
narrationScript: Visual testing keeps its physics constant and changes its setup for every component it meets. This lesson tours the nine applications the Basic outline names. Welds get staged inspection: joint preparation, root opening, and cleanliness before the arc, each pass during, and the finished bead after, measured with gauges for undercut, reinforcement, and fillet size. Pressure vessels are walked externally for corrosion, bulging, and leaking seams, then examined internally through manways, or through nozzles with a borescope that must resolve at least as well as the unaided eye. In pumps, cavitation prints spongy pitting at the impeller vane inlets. In valves, leakage flow wire-draws smooth channels across the seating surfaces. Bolting cracks where stress concentrates, at the head-to-shank fillet and the first engaged thread. Castings show cold shuts and hot tears. Forgings show laps near the parting line, while their bursts hide inside, beyond any eye. Extrusions show die lines and transverse speed cracks. And microcircuits move the whole exercise under the stereo microscope, where bond wires and metallization are judged at magnifications the unaided eye cannot touch.

sections.heading: Nine Applications, One Discipline
sections.body:
The previous lesson built the visual method: light, the eye, resolution, direct versus remote technique, and the equipment that extends each. This lesson is where that machinery goes to work. The CP-105 Basic outline names nine specific applications — **metal joining processes, pressure vessels, pumps, valves, bolting, castings, forgings, extrusions, and microcircuits** — and what changes from one to the next is not the physics but the **setup**: how the eye reaches the surface, how much light and magnification the job needs, and which discontinuities the component's manufacturing process and service history make likely.

What does *not* change are the ground rules. For **direct visual examination**, ASME Section V, Article 9 sets the geometry every application inherits: the eye within **600 mm (24 in.)** of the surface, at an angle **not less than 30 degrees** to it, with a minimum of **100 footcandles (1076 lux)** of illumination at the surface for the detection and evaluation of small anomalies. Where the component's geometry defeats that — a vessel that cannot be entered, a valve still in the line, a pump that stays assembled — **remote visual examination** takes over with mirrors, borescopes, fiberscopes, and video systems, under one non-negotiable condition: the remote system must have **resolution capability at least equivalent to direct visual observation**.

In an FAA Part 145 repair station, VT is the gatekeeper: nearly every part receives a documented visual examination before any other NDT method touches it, and many components are accepted or rejected on VT alone. A Level III preparing for the Basic exam needs each application at **familiarity level** — what the setup looks like, and which discontinuities the eye is hunting.

sections.heading: Metal Joining Processes: Before, During, and After the Arc
sections.body:
Welding is VT's highest-leverage application because the method works in **three stages**, and the earlier a problem is caught, the cheaper it is.

- **Before welding.** Fit-up inspection verifies the **root opening, root face, and groove angle** against the welding procedure; **alignment (hi-lo)** across the joint; **cleanliness** — bare, dry metal free of oil, paint, rust, and moisture; and the condition of base metal and consumables. A bad fit-up reliably manufactures incomplete penetration and slag traps that no amount of welder skill repairs later.
- **During welding.** The eye follows the work pass by pass: **root pass contour and fusion at the toes**, **interpass cleaning** (slag removed before the next pass buries it), distortion and joint movement, and general conformance to the procedure.
- **After welding.** The finished bead is measured, not admired. **Fillet weld gauges** check leg size, throat, and convexity or concavity; **bridge cam and undercut gauges** measure reinforcement height, undercut depth, and misalignment. The surface discontinuity list is the exam's favorite: **undercut** at the toes, **overlap** (unfused metal rolled over the base material), **underfill**, **excessive reinforcement**, **surface porosity**, **crater and surface cracks**, **arc strikes**, and **spatter**.

For aerospace hardware, the acceptance criteria typically come from **AWS D17.1**, the fusion welding specification for aerospace applications; structural steel work cites AWS D1.1, and pressure parts cite the ASME codes. **Brazed and soldered joints** get their own visual logic: the eye verifies **wetting and a continuous, smooth fillet** of filler metal at the joint line — non-wetting, voids in the fillet, and corrosive flux residue are the rejectable signatures.

sections.caption: Staged weld VT. At fit-up the eye verifies root opening, groove angle, alignment, and cleanliness; during welding it follows root contour and interpass cleaning; after welding the gauges come out — reinforcement, undercut, profile, and the surface discontinuity hunt.

sections.heading: Pressure Vessels, Pumps, and Valves: Eyes Inside the Pressure Boundary
sections.body:
**Pressure vessels** are examined visually twice in their lives: during construction, when the fabricator's program verifies weld quality and dimensions, and then repeatedly **in service**, where programs built on documents such as **API 510** lean on VT as the primary method. The external walkdown hunts **corrosion and pitting**, staining that betrays **leakage at seams and nozzles**, **bulging or distortion** (overheating or overpressure), **blistering** (hydrogen damage), damaged insulation that invites corrosion under it, and the condition of **supports, saddles, and foundations**. Internal examination is direct where a **manway** admits the inspector, and remote where it does not — a **borescope or videoscope through a nozzle** sweeps the shell, the weld seams, and the low points where corrosion concentrates.

**Pumps** concentrate their visual evidence on the wetted parts. The classic find is **cavitation damage**: collapsing vapor bubbles hammer the impeller surface into **spongy, deeply pitted metal**, concentrated near the **vane inlets and low-pressure faces**. The eye also hunts **erosion** from entrained solids, corrosion of the casing, **scoring of shaft sleeves and wear rings**, and damage at sealing surfaces. With the casing opened the work is direct; with the pump assembled, a borescope through the **suction port** reaches the impeller eye.

**Valves** add one signature every Level III should name on sight: **wire drawing** — smooth, wormhole-like channels cut across the **seat and disc seating surfaces** by high-velocity leakage flow when a valve is operated nearly closed or left passing. The eye also finds **galling** on stems and threads, erosion of the body where flow changes direction, corrosion, and deposits that prevent full seating. Bonnet removal exposes the trim directly; a borescope through the **line ports** examines seats with the valve still in the piping.

sections.title: Remote means equivalent — or it does not count
sections.body: Every access trick in this lesson — borescope through a nozzle, videoscope to a valve seat, mirror behind a flange — stands on one rule from ASME Section V, Article 9: a remote system must deliver **resolution at least equivalent to direct viewing**, and direct viewing itself is defined: eye within **600 mm (24 in.)**, angle **no less than 30 degrees**, **100 fc (1076 lux)** on the surface. Demonstrating that the system can resolve a fine line or test character at the working distance is how the equivalence is proved — an image that merely *shows* the seat without resolving what direct VT would resolve is not an examination.

sections.caption: Access decides the technique. A vessel offers a manway for direct entry and nozzles for the borescope; a valve offers its bonnet opening and line ports; a pump offers its suction port. In each case the route must put the eye — or its remote equivalent — on the damage zone with direct-VT resolution and lighting.

sections.heading: Bolting, Castings, Forgings, and Extrusions: Read the Process in the Surface
sections.body:
**Bolting.** A threaded fastener concentrates stress at two places — the **head-to-shank fillet** and the **first engaged thread** — and that is where fatigue cracks live. Visual examination of bolting covers **thread damage** (nicks, crossed or flattened threads), **corrosion and pitting**, damaged protective plating, **galling** from assembly, bent shanks, and **stretch**: necking of the shank or elongated thread pitch, checked against a pitch gauge. A stretched or necked fastener has yielded and is scrap — in aviation maintenance, suspect bolts are replaced, never reworked.

**Castings.** The as-cast surface records the pour. **Cold shuts** appear as smooth, curved linear indications where two streams of metal met without fusing; **hot tears** are ragged cracks formed during solidification at changes of section; **shrinkage** shows as surface depressions or spongy areas at heavy sections; **gas porosity** prints smooth, rounded holes; **sand inclusions and scabs** roughen the surface; a **misrun** leaves an incompletely filled, round-edged void.

**Forgings.** Hot working writes different signatures. **Laps** are surface metal folded over without fusion — smooth-bottomed, lying at a shallow angle to the surface, classically near the **flash line or parting line**. **Seams** are tight longitudinal discontinuities carried forward from defects in the original billet. Scale pits and underfill round out the visual list — but **forging bursts are internal** ruptures from improper hot working, and no visual examination will find them: that is volumetric territory, ultrasonic testing's job.

**Extrusions.** The die leaves its autograph: **die lines** — fine longitudinal scoring from die wear and buildup; **pickup or galling** where metal adhered to the die and smeared the surface; **blisters** from subsurface gas; and **transverse speed cracks** when the extrusion ran too hot or too fast.

sections.heading: Microcircuits: VT at the Bottom of the Magnification Ladder's Far End
sections.body:
Microcircuit inspection is still visual testing — light, optics, a trained eye, documented criteria — but the features of interest are measured in **micrometers**, so the unaided-eye rules give way to the **stereo microscope**. Military and space-grade practice follows **MIL-STD-883** visual inspection methods: package-level examination at low magnification (around **10x**), internal **pre-cap inspection** of the open device at roughly **30x to 60x** for wire bonds and die attach, and **75x to 150x** for die metallization. Pre-cap matters because it is the last look: once the lid is sealed, only radiography sees inside.

The inspection targets map one-to-one onto failure mechanisms:

- **Wire bonds** — placement on the bond pad, bond deformation, necked or nicked wires, excessive or missing tails, sagging loops that could short to adjacent conductors.
- **Die attach** — a continuous fillet of attach material; voids and cracks that compromise the mechanical and thermal path.
- **Metallization** — scratches and voids that narrow conductors, corrosion, and bridging between adjacent traces.
- **Foreign material** — loose conductive particles, contamination, flux residue; on assembled boards, solder joint quality (wetting, cold joints, bridging) is judged to workmanship standards such as IPC-A-610.

The setup is as controlled as the criteria: **shadow-free diffuse illumination**, ESD-safe grounding and handling, cleanliness discipline, and no bare-hand contact. The lesson of this application is the span of the method itself — the same discipline that gauges a weld bead at arm's length judges a one-mil bond wire at 60x, and both stand or fall on access, lighting, and resolution.

sections.caption: Nine VT applications: the setup that reaches the surface, and the discontinuities the eye hunts there.
rows: Metal joining (welds, brazes)
Direct VT staged before, during, and after welding; fillet, bridge cam, and undercut gauges
Fit-up errors; undercut, overlap, underfill, excess reinforcement, surface porosity, cracks, arc strikes; braze fillet wetting and continuity
Pressure vessels
External walkdown; internal entry through manways or borescope through nozzles (API 510 programs)
Corrosion and pitting, leakage staining at seams and nozzles, bulging, blistering, support and insulation condition
Pumps
Opened casing for direct VT, or borescope through the suction port to the impeller eye
Spongy cavitation pitting at vane inlets, erosion from solids, scoring of sleeves and wear rings
Valves
Bonnet removal for direct trim examination, or borescope through line ports with the valve in place
Wire drawing across seating surfaces, stem and thread galling, body erosion, deposits preventing seating
Bolting
Removed fastener under direct light, low-power magnifier, thread pitch gauge
Cracks at head-to-shank fillet and first engaged thread, thread damage, stretch and necking, corrosion, galling
Castings
Direct VT of the as-cast surface
Cold shuts, hot tears, shrinkage, gas porosity, sand inclusions, misruns
Forgings
Direct VT with attention at the flash and parting lines
Laps and seams, scale pits, underfill — internal bursts belong to volumetric methods
Extrusions
Direct VT along the extruded length
Die lines, pickup and galling, blisters, transverse speed cracks
Microcircuits
Stereo microscope, roughly 10x to 150x per MIL-STD-883 methods; ESD-safe, clean handling
Wire bond and die attach defects, metallization scratches and voids, loose particles, contamination, solder joint quality

figures.title: Inspection access points on common components

interactive.title: Match the VT Setup to the Component
interactive.intro: Eight inspection jobs land on your bench and in your hangar. Sort each one to the visual setup that actually reaches the surface: direct VT, remote VT through an access opening, or magnified VT under optics.
buckets: Remote VT — borescope through an access opening
Magnified VT — loupe or stereo microscope

items.label: Completed fillet welds on an engine mount, with leg size and convexity to verify
items.why: An accessible finished weld is direct VT's home turf: eye within 600 mm at 30 degrees or better, 100 fc on the surface, and a fillet gauge to turn the look into a measurement against AWS D17.1-type criteria.

items.label: Interior shell seams of a pressure vessel that cannot be entered, examined through a 100 mm nozzle
items.why: No entry means remote technique: a borescope or videoscope through the nozzle sweeps the seams and low points — valid only if its resolution is demonstrated at least equivalent to direct viewing.

items.label: Wire bonds and die attach on an open hybrid microcircuit before the lid is sealed
items.why: Pre-cap inspection per MIL-STD-883-style methods runs at roughly 30x to 60x under a stereo microscope — bond placement, necked wires, and die-attach fillets are invisible to the unaided eye.

items.label: Seat and disc sealing surfaces of a globe valve left in the line, suspected of passing flow
items.why: With the valve still in the piping, a borescope through the line ports reaches the seating surfaces to look for wire drawing — the smooth channels cut by high-velocity leakage flow.

items.label: Threads, first engaged thread region, and head-to-shank fillet of removed engine bolts
items.why: Fastener fatigue starts at the stress concentrations, and incipient cracks and thread damage there are best screened under a low-power loupe, with a pitch gauge checking for stretch.

items.label: Weld joint fit-up: root opening, groove angle, and hi-lo alignment before the first arc
items.why: Fit-up inspection is direct VT with measuring tools — the cheapest moment in the weld's life to reject it, before incomplete penetration is manufactured into the root.

items.label: Impeller vane inlets of an assembled centrifugal pump, suspected of cavitation damage
items.why: Without opening the casing, the suction port is the route: a borescope to the impeller eye looks for the spongy, deeply pitted surfaces cavitation leaves at the vane inlets.

items.label: As-cast surfaces of a pump housing casting, checked for cold shuts and sand inclusions
items.why: Large, accessible as-cast surfaces are walked directly under good light — cold shuts print as smooth curved linear indications, sand inclusions as rough patches, both within unaided-eye resolution.

checkQuestions.question: A welding inspector's most economical opportunity to prevent a rejectable weld is:
options: After the final pass, when gauges can measure the finished bead
At fit-up — verifying root opening, groove angle, alignment, and cleanliness before the first arc
During the hydrostatic test of the completed assembly
After painting, when surface contrast is at its best
checkQuestions.explanation: Weld VT is staged precisely because cost climbs with time: a bad root opening or contaminated bevel caught at fit-up costs a regrind, while the incomplete penetration it manufactures costs an excavation after the cap — or a failed component after the hydro. Gauging the finished bead confirms quality; fit-up inspection prevents the defect, and paint actually hides surface discontinuities.

checkQuestions.question: During examination of a disassembled globe valve, the inspector finds smooth, wormhole-like channels cut across the seating surfaces of the seat and disc. The classic cause is:
options: Hydrogen blistering of the seat material
A casting cold shut exposed by machining
Wire drawing — high-velocity leakage flow eroding the seating surfaces while the valve was throttled nearly closed or left passing
Cavitation in the upstream pump casing
checkQuestions.explanation: Wire drawing is the valve-trim signature: fluid forced through a nearly closed or leaking seat at high velocity cuts smooth erosion channels across the seating surfaces. Cavitation prints spongy pitting on impeller vane inlets, hydrogen blistering raises bulges on plate exposed to a hydrogen charging environment, and a cold shut is a smooth curved fusion line from casting — different components, different signatures.

checkQuestions.question: A vessel's internal shell welds must be examined, but entry is impossible, so a videoscope through a nozzle is proposed. For the remote examination to be valid, the governing requirement is that the remote system:
options: Magnify the surface by at least 10x
Have resolution capability at least equivalent to that of direct visual examination
Use black-and-white imaging to improve contrast
Be inserted no farther than 600 mm into the vessel
checkQuestions.explanation: ASME Section V, Article 9 defines direct VT — eye within 600 mm (24 in.), angle no less than 30 degrees, 100 fc (1076 lux) on the surface — and requires any remote technique to deliver resolution at least equivalent to that direct view. Magnification alone proves nothing about resolution, color is not restricted, and the 600 mm figure is the direct-viewing eye-to-surface distance, not an insertion limit.

checkQuestions.question: Which pairing of product form and characteristic surface discontinuity is correct?
options: Forging — gas porosity from trapped mold moisture
Extrusion — hot tear at a heavy-to-thin section change
Casting — lap folded into the surface at the flash line
Casting — cold shut where two streams of solidifying metal met without fusing
checkQuestions.explanation: Cold shuts, hot tears, shrinkage, gas porosity, and sand inclusions are casting-borne — they record the pour and solidification. Laps ride forging flash and parting lines, where surface metal folds over without fusing; die lines and transverse speed cracks belong to extrusions. Each manufacturing process writes its own discontinuity list, and the exam tests whether you can read the surface back to the process.

checkQuestions.question: Visual inspection of microcircuit die-level features such as wire bonds and metallization is performed:
options: With the unaided eye, provided illumination reaches 1076 lux
Only after the package lid is hermetically sealed
Under a stereo microscope at magnifications from roughly 30x up to 150x per MIL-STD-883-type methods, before the package is sealed
With a fiberscope inserted through the package wall
checkQuestions.explanation: Die-level features are micrometer-scale, so pre-cap internal inspection runs at roughly 30x to 60x for wire bonds and die attach and 75x to 150x for metallization, under MIL-STD-883 visual methods with ESD-safe, clean handling. The examination must happen before sealing — afterward only radiography sees inside — and the unaided-eye lighting rule governs macro-scale VT, not features the eye cannot resolve at any illumination.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Basic Topical Outline §2.10.2 (VT specific applications: metal joining processes, pressure vessels, pumps, valves, bolting, castings, forgings, extrusions, microcircuits)
ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section V, Article 9 (direct and remote visual examination requirements, summarized)
AWS D17.1, Specification for Fusion Welding for Aerospace Applications (weld visual acceptance context, summarized)
API 510, Pressure Vessel Inspection Code (in-service vessel VT context, summarized)
MIL-STD-883, Test Methods 2010 and 2017 (microcircuit internal visual inspection, summarized)
